questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of fever, cough, coryza and conjunctivitis. He has a fine maculopapular rash on his face and trunk. He received one dose of MMR at 12 months but missed the second dose. On examination, you note a few Koplik spots on the buccal mucosa. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Koplik spots are pathognomonic for measles. The prodrome of cough, coryza, conjunctivitis (the 3 Cs) is classic. Rubella has milder symptoms and post-auricular lymphadenopathy; Kawasaki disease has persistent fever and conjunctivitis but no Koplik spots and a different rash; scarlet fever has a sandpaper rash and strawberry tongue; roseola has high fever then rash after defervescence.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant is brought to the emergency department with episodes of apnoea and cyanosis. There is no cough or fever. The mother reports poor feeding for a week. The infant is not immunised. A full septic screen is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Pertussis in young infants can present with apnoea and cyanosis without the classic paroxysmal cough due to immature respiratory control. RSV bronchiolitis typically has cough and wheeze; meningitis has fever and irritability; sepsis would have positive cultures or other signs; congenital heart disease may have murmurs or cyanosis but not typically apnoeic episodes.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy presents with acute onset of right testicular pain and swelling. He has no urinary symptoms. He has mild parotid swelling on the left. He received only one dose of MMR vaccine. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Parotid swelling preceding testicular pain is classic for mumps orchitis. Testicular torsion presents with sudden severe pain and absent cremasteric reflex; epididymo-orchitis usually has dysuria or urethral discharge; haematocele has history of trauma; hydrocele is painless and transilluminates.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl presents with fever, vomiting, and a rapidly spreading petechial rash. She is drowsy and hypotensive. She has not completed her immunisations. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of fever, sepsis, and non-blanching purpuric rash is typical of meningococcaemia. HSP has palpable purpura on lower limbs with joint/abdo symptoms; ITP has petechiae but no fever; HUS follows diarrhoea; leukaemia has pallor and bruising but not acute fever and hypotension.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant presents with acute onset of stridor, fever, and drooling. He is sitting forward, anxious. He has not received any vaccines. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic presentation of acute epiglottitis includes the 4 Ds: dysphagia, drooling, distress, and stridor, with a toxic child. This is usually due to Hib, preventable by vaccine. Croup has a barking cough and low-grade fever; bacterial tracheitis has purulent secretions; foreign body has sudden onset with no fever; peritonsillar abscess has muffled voice and trismus.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old unvaccinated child presents with fever, coryza, conjunctivitis, and a maculopapular rash that started on the face. Koplik spots are visible. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Measles PCR from a throat swab is the most sensitive and specific confirmatory test in the acute phase. IgM serology can be negative in the first few days and may be affected by prior vaccination.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant presents with paroxysms of coughing, post-tussive vomiting, and inspiratory whoop. The mother reports the cough started 2 weeks ago. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Nasopharyngeal swab for Bordetella pertussis PCR
questions.explanation: PCR from a nasopharyngeal swab is the diagnostic test of choice in infants and young children, as serology is unreliable under 1 year of age. Culture is less sensitive and slower.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old child with a BCG scar has a positive tuberculin skin test (15 mm induration) during school screening. The child is asymptomatic with a normal chest X-ray. Which test should be performed next to confirm latent TB infection?
options: Sputum for AFB smear and culture
questions.explanation: IGRA is specific for Mycobacterium tuberculosis and is not affected by prior BCG vaccination, unlike the tuberculin skin test. A positive IGRA confirms latent TB infection.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old presents with fever, a non-blanching purpuric rash, and signs of meningitis. Blood cultures were drawn but the child had already received antibiotics in the community. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Blood PCR for meningococcus is highly sensitive and remains positive even after antibiotics, unlike culture. CSF PCR is also effective but lumbar puncture may be delayed.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant presents with intermittent screaming, drawing up of legs, and an episode of red-currant jelly stool. The symptoms began 3 days after receiving the rotavirus vaccine. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line imaging for suspected intussusception, with high sensitivity and no radiation exposure. It can demonstrate a target sign. X-ray may be non-specific.

questions.stem: A 13-month-old child who has never received any vaccinations presents to clinic. The parents now consent to immunisation. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Follow the UK catch-up schedule as per the Green Book
Give only the MMR vaccine today
Start the primary schedule from 8 weeks intervals as per the 8-week schedule
Schedule separate appointments for each vaccine at monthly intervals
Give all routine vaccines at the same appointment today
questions.explanation: The UK catch-up schedule (Green Book) provides guidance for children who miss vaccines. Giving all vaccines at once risks adverse reactions and is not recommended; catch-up involves spacing doses appropriately.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child with a history of immediate allergic reaction to egg (but not anaphylaxis) is due for the MMR vaccine. According to UK guidelines, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Give a reduced dose of MMR
Administer MMR in a hospital setting with resuscitation equipment
Refer for skin-prick testing to egg before MMR
Avoid MMR and consider monovalent measles vaccine
Administer MMR in primary care as routine
questions.explanation: UK guidelines state that MMR is safe in children with egg allergy, including anaphylaxis, and can be given in primary care without special precautions. No testing or hospital setting is needed.

questions.stem: A 12-month-old child had a febrile seizure 6 days after receiving the first dose of MMR. The parents are worried about the second dose due at 3 years 4 months. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Administer the second dose under hospital supervision
Give only the measles component of MMR
Give the second dose as per routine schedule
Delay the second dose until after age 5
questions.explanation: Febrile seizures after MMR are not a contraindication to further doses; the second dose should be given at the routine age. The seizure is likely febrile, not allergic, and the benefit of vaccination outweighs the risk.

questions.stem: A preterm infant born at 30 weeks gestation is now 8 weeks old (chronological age) and medically stable. When should the first routine vaccinations be given?
options: At 12 weeks chronological age to allow for prematurity
Two weeks after discharge from the neonatal unit
At 40 weeks post-conceptual age (term corrected age)
Half doses at 8 weeks, then full doses later
questions.explanation: UK guidelines recommend immunising preterm infants at chronological age from 8 weeks, regardless of gestational age, provided they are clinically stable. Corrected age is not used for vaccination timing.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant was diagnosed with severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) at birth and is awaiting stem cell transplant. Which routine vaccination is contraindicated at this age?
options: Hepatitis B vaccine (if given as separate dose)
questions.explanation: Rotavirus vaccine is a live oral vaccine and is contraindicated in infants with SCID due to risk of severe infection. The other vaccines are non-live and can be given, though expert advice may be sought.

questions.stem: A 14-week-old infant born at 28 weeks gestation attends for routine immunisations. The parents are concerned about giving vaccines at this early chronological age. The infant is well, feeding, and gaining weight. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Delay vaccines until the infant reaches 8 weeks corrected.
Check lymphocyte subsets and immunoglobulin levels.
Administer vaccines according to corrected age (i.e., 6 weeks corrected).
Administer vaccines according to chronological age.
Refer to paediatric immunology for an individualised schedule.
questions.explanation: Preterm infants should be immunised at the same chronological age as term infants, regardless of birth weight or gestation, because the risk of disease outweighs the theoretical risk of lower immunogenicity.

questions.stem: A 15-week-old infant is due for routine immunisations including the first dose of rotavirus vaccine. He has a history of intussusception treated surgically at 12 weeks. He is now well. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform an abdominal ultrasound before vaccination.
Refer to a paediatric surgeon for clearance.
Give rotavirus vaccine with a one-month delay.
questions.explanation: Rotavirus vaccine is contraindicated in infants with a history of intussusception due to the risk of recurrence. The vaccine should not be given.

questions.stem: A 12-month-old child with nephrotic syndrome is maintained on prednisolone 20 mg daily for the past 3 weeks. He is due for MMR and Hib/MenC booster. He has no fever or acute illness. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Administer Hib/MenC but withhold MMR until steroids are stopped.
Check varicella zoster IgG before any vaccinations.
Refer to paediatric nephrology for advice.
questions.explanation: High-dose systemic corticosteroids (>20 mg prednisolone per day for ≥2 weeks) are a contraindication to live vaccines such as MMR. Inactivated vaccines like Hib/MenC can be given. MMR should be deferred until at least 3 months after steroids are discontinued.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant is brought for first immunisations. The mother says the baby had a mild cough and runny nose yesterday but is now well with no fever. Examination is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check full blood count and C-reactive protein.
questions.explanation: Minor illness without fever is not a contraindication to vaccination. Vaccines can be given as scheduled.

questions.stem: A 5-month-old infant who has received no previous immunisations is brought for catch-up. The parents are keen to start today. The infant is well. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Administer only the hexavalent vaccine today and wait until 6 months for others.
Refer to a paediatric immunisation clinic.
Administer all overdue vaccines including the first dose of rotavirus.
Administer hexavalent, PCV, and MenB but not rotavirus.
Check hepatitis B serology before any vaccination.
questions.explanation: For rotavirus vaccine, the first dose must be given <15 weeks of age and the last dose <24 weeks. At 20 weeks (5 months), the first dose is too old, so rotavirus cannot be given now. The other vaccines are not age-limited and should be given according to the catch-up schedule.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant becomes pale and floppy 15 minutes after receiving the 6-in-1 vaccine. The nurse observes generalised urticaria. Heart rate is 170/min, respiratory rate 40/min, and oxygen saturation 94% in air. Which single finding most clearly indicates a life-threatening anaphylactic reaction requiring immediate escalation?
questions.explanation: Generalised urticaria is a hallmark of anaphylaxis and distinguishes it from vasovagal syncope, which does not involve histamine-mediated skin changes. Tachycardia and hypotonia can occur in vasovagal reactions, and mild desaturation may be secondary to hypoventilation. Urticaria is the specific red flag for anaphylaxis.

questions.stem: A 13-month-old infant presented to the emergency department with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes, which self-terminated, 9 days after receiving the MMR vaccine. On arrival, the child is drowsy but rousable, with no focal neurological signs. Which single finding would most strongly indicate a serious post-vaccination encephalopathy rather than a simple febrile seizure?
questions.explanation: Prolonged postictal drowsiness (>30 minutes) suggests encephalopathy rather than a simple febrile seizure, where children typically recover quickly. A 3-minute seizure and the timing (5–12 days post-MMR) are consistent with febrile seizures; fever and age are typical for febrile seizures. The drowsiness is the discriminating feature for encephalopathy.

questions.stem: A 1-year-old infant with a history of recurrent bacterial infections and failure to thrive is brought for the first MMR vaccine. On examination, no tonsillar tissue is visible, and no lymph nodes are palpable. Which single finding is an absolute contraindication to giving a live attenuated vaccine?
questions.explanation: Absent tonsils and lymph nodes in this context are clinical signs of severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID), which is an absolute contraindication to live vaccines such as MMR. Failure to thrive and recurrent infections are suggestive but not specific. Oral thrush can occur in milder immunodeficiencies but does not alone contraindicate vaccination.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of paroxysmal coughing spells, followed by episodes of cyanosis. During a coughing spell, the infant becomes limp and stops breathing for 15 seconds, requiring tactile stimulation to recover. Which single finding is the most urgent red flag for impending respiratory failure in this context?
questions.explanation: Apnoea requiring stimulation indicates severe pertussis with risk of respiratory failure, especially in young infants. Cyanosis and limpness are concerning but reversible; apnoea that needs intervention signals impending need for respiratory support. Age <3 months is a risk factor but not a red flag in itself.

questions.stem: An immunocompetent 4-month-old infant who received BCG vaccine at birth now has a 2 cm ulcer at the vaccination site and a 1.5 cm mobile, non-tender ipsilateral axillary lymph node. The child remains well with no fever and good weight gain. Which single additional finding would raise suspicion of disseminated BCG infection and require urgent specialist referral?
questions.explanation: Fever and hepatomegaly are systemic signs that suggest disseminated BCG infection (BCGosis), a life-threatening complication in immunodeficient children. Local ulceration and regional lymphadenopathy are common benign reactions. Normal weight gain and lack of tenderness are reassuring.
